text:
Biomass burning (BB) emissions of non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) affect secondary organic aerosol (SOA) modeling and assessment of the resulting aerosol impact on regional air quality and global climate. Here, we present the data set that characterizes the emission of gaseous organics from combustion of six globally important types of vegetation fuels, namely conifer branches, agricultural residues, peat, and grasses that were burnt in the Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4) facility. Organic compounds from the smoke of these fires were collected on sorbent cartridges and analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). The ability to take full advantage of the enhanced detection limits and peak resolution properties of GC × GC–ToFMS has enabled the largest number of species ever reported for BB emissions and the corresponding data set contains 708 organic species that have been identified or tentatively identified.

The analysis of biomass burning (BB) and wildland fires emissions has revealed a high degree of chemical diversity among different fuels and a large number of isomers for many species. Specifically, 38 isomers of monoterpene were identified in the emissions from burning coniferous fuels, and the profile of these isomers matched closely with that of plant species-specific essential oils, providing a new proxy to constrain the estimated emissions of terpenes. In addition, 11 isomers of sesquiterpenes, which are the high-yield precursors to SOA in BB smoke, were speciated for the first time. The emission factors of all of the identified species were calculated and interpreted within the context of their potential to contribute to secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation. By detailing a large number of intermediate-volatility and reactive organic compounds (IVOCs and ROCs), the study helps to constrain the large uncertainties associated with the development of emission inventories and provides an expansive data set of detailed species information that can be used to develop improved photochemical models of BB plumes.
explanation: Polished, domain-specific abstract with plausible experimental details; it could be human-authored, but advanced AI could also generate such text, so a moderate probability is estimated.
